paragraphs: The resettlement movement is not monolithic. It encompasses pragmatic land advocates who argue for Israeli military control without immediate civilian settlement; theological nationalists who believe Jewish settlement of the entire biblical land of Israel is a divine command; and former settlers who experienced the 2005 disengagement as a personal trauma and seek restoration.

Experts interviewed for this report describe the movement as politically influential beyond its numbers, capable of constraining Israeli government policy even when it cannot dictate it. Its advocates have successfully placed resettlement on the agenda of Knesset committees, secured government funding for feasibility studies, and built organizational infrastructure that did not exist before October 7.

The practical obstacles remain enormous. Gaza's physical infrastructure has been devastated by months of intensive warfare. The humanitarian situation is catastrophic. International consensus against resettlement is nearly universal. And polls suggest that even within Israel, a majority of Jewish Israelis oppose settling civilians in Gaza.

Yet the movement persists — and its advocates point to what they describe as changing facts on the ground: prolonged Israeli military presence, the collapse of Hamas governance, and a diplomatic environment in which the United States has shown greater tolerance for Israeli unilateral action. For a movement accustomed to the margins, these are not trivial openings.
keyVoices: Bezalel Smotrich, Finance Minister, Religious Zionism party
Daniella Weiss, former Gush Katif resident and Nahala movement leader
Yossi Dagan, Samaria Regional Council head and advocacy organizer
Francesca Albanese, UN Special Rapporteur on the Palestinian territories
Yair Lapid, Israeli opposition leader, Yesh Atid party
Matthew Miller, US State Department spokesperson
relatedCoverage: Gaza's reconstruction: who decides the future?

methodology: This report is based on interviews conducted between October 2024 and January 2026, analysis of Israeli parliamentary records, advocacy organization documents, polling data from the Israel Democracy Institute, and monitoring of public statements by Israeli officials. All statistics are drawn from publicly available sources cited in each chart. Visual Desk editorial standards apply.
